steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction with gallstone pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: The outside MRI and the plan note mention prior imaging findings, but at this institution no formal ultrasound has yet been performed; the physician needs direct sonographic characterization of the gallbladder, biliary tree, and pancreatic head to confirm gallstone pancreatitis, assess for choledocholithiasis, and evaluate for acute cholecystitis as a co-existing or primary process.
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstone in the gallbladder neck or common bile duct, gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid indicating cholecystitis, and/or a dilated common bile duct suggesting biliary obstruction driving the elevated bilirubin and transaminases.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The history of a gallbladder neck stone, markedly elevated lipase (1844), rising total bilirubin (4.2), and significant transaminitis (AST 315, ALT 385, ALP 408) all point to a biliary etiology; a dedicated right-upper-quadrant/liver-gallbladder ultrasound is the first-line modality to confirm cholelithiasis, assess the common bile duct diameter, and evaluate for cholecystitis before deciding between ERCP and surgical intervention.
grounding: History: 'US revealed a stone in the neck of the gallbladder, distended gallbladder without pericholecystic fluid, and pancreatic inflammation'
History: 'Lipase was elevated to 6000 with tbili at 2.6 and mild transaminitis'
History: 'Plan: CT A/P, pain management as needed, c/s ERCP vs ACS'
Imaging already resulted BEFORE this decision point: '(none — only the baseline workup below was available)'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient presents with biochemical evidence of gallstone pancreatitis (lipase 1844, transaminitis, hyperbilirubinemia) and a history from an outside hospital describing a gallbladder neck stone and pancreatic inflammation, but no imaging has yet been formally performed at this institution. I am ordering a dedicated gallbladder/liver ultrasound to confirm the stone burden, measure the common bile duct diameter (to assess for choledocholithiasis driving the elevated bilirubin), and evaluate for concurrent acute cholecystitis — findings that will directly determine whether urgent ERCP, cholecystectomy, or both are needed.

vindication.actual_finding: Ultrasound demonstrated a 10 mm gallstone at the gallbladder neck with gallbladder wall edema, a mildly dilated CBD measuring 9 mm with nonshadowing echogenic material (likely sludge), and no intrahepatic biliary dilation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly confirmed the expected findings of a gallstone at the gallbladder neck, gallbladder wall thickening/edema suggesting cholecystitis, and a dilated CBD with biliary material consistent with obstruction driving the elevated bilirubin and transaminases.
